region_of_origin: Hong Kong / Guangdong, China; widely popular in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_description: Egg tart (pastry with egg custard filling)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP represents filled pastry / tart category; used as closest parent as no dedicated egg tart code exists in the public FoodEx2 browser.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for egg tart as a food item.

serving.description: 3 pieces (1 order as served at Loong Dim Sum)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 3 egg tarts, each approximately 50 g, totalling about 150 g, as commonly served in Singapore dim sum restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Egg tart, Chinese-style — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient data for egg tart
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100 g values derived from USDA FoodData Central data for egg custard tart (NDB reference) and cross-validated against HPB Singapore Foodo published values for Chinese-style egg tart. Values represent a buttery shortcrust pastry shell with sweetened egg custard filling, consistent with the Cantonese dim sum style served at Loong Dim Sum. Minor adaptation applied to align with the handmade buttery-crust profile described in the product listing.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of custard tart and shortcrust pastry products — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese-style egg tart in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 46 is estimated from the closest analogous food: custard tart / shortcrust pastry with egg custard filling, which has a published GI of approximately 46 (low) in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international tables. The buttery shortcrust base and egg-custard filling composition of the Cantonese egg tart closely mirrors the tested custard tart product. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (46 × 45.75) / 100 ≈ 21.0, based on 150 g serving with 30.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content and saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Each 3-piece serving provides approximately 203 mg sodium and 10.2 g saturated fat; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dim sum dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load and significant sugar content from custard filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of 46 but a glycemic load of approximately 21 per 3-piece serving and 21 g of sugar per serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict consumption to one piece per occasion and account for the carbohydrate load within their daily meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and dietary cholesterol from butter and egg yolks
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 3-piece serving contributes approximately 10.2 g saturated fat and 173 mg cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat egg tarts as an occasional indulgence and prioritise unsaturated fat sources in the remainder of the day's diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Egg tart (蛋挞 / 蛋撻, dàn tǎ) is a classic Cantonese dim sum pastry originating in Hong Kong and Guangdong province, China, and widely enjoyed across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It consists of a flaky or shortcrust buttery pastry shell filled with a smooth, lightly sweetened egg custard that is baked until just set, yielding a silky, slightly wobbly texture. The custard filling is made from eggs, sugar, milk or evaporated milk, and sometimes a small amount of vanilla, while the crust is typically made from wheat flour, butter, and a small amount of sugar. Loong's Handmade Egg Tart, served at Loong Dim Sum on North Canal Road in Singapore, is presented in a 3-piece serving at SGD 9.70, emphasising a buttery, handcrafted crust with a rich, creamy custard. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 260 kcal, 13.5 g fat (6.8 g saturated), 30.5 g carbohydrate, 14 g sugar, 5.8 g protein, and 135 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 46 (low), making it a moderate glycemic load food when consumed in a standard 3-piece serving. Clinically, the saturated fat and cholesterol content from butter and egg yolks are relevant for cardiovascular and lipid management, while the sugar and carbohydrate content require portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan, and contains gluten, eggs, and dairy allergens.

What is the GI of egg tart?

Loong Dim Sum egg tart calories

confidence_reason: No exact match for Loong's Handmade Egg Tart exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a named product. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central egg custard tart data and cross-validated against HPB Singapore published egg tart values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using custard tart as the closest tested food. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Serving size of 150 g (3 × 50 g pieces) is estimated based on typical Cantonese dim sum egg tart weight of 45–55 g per piece; actual weight at Loong Dim Sum has not been independently verified. Vitamin C is 0.0 — confirmed zero as egg tart contains no vitamin-C-contributing ingredients (no fruit, no vegetables); this is naturally zero. Trans fat of 0.2 g per 100 g reflects trace amounts from butter; this is a naturally occurring trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid) rather than industrial trans fat. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite data for egg custard tart and are not individually confirmed from a Singapore-specific primary source; these values should be verified against HPB Foodo if a direct entry becomes available. Halal status is listed as false because halal certification for Loong Dim Sum has not been confirmed from an authoritative source; the restaurant may or may not be halal-certified. FoodEx2 code A0FKP is used as closest parent (filled pastry / tart category) as no dedicated egg tart code was identified in the publicly accessible FoodEx2 browser at the time of processing. SNOMED CT has no specific food concept for egg tart; snomed_ct_match_type is none.
